info.text: In this demo I’ve imported Two.js a lightweight drawing library by designer Jono and many others. Part of what makes it unique from other drawing libraries is that it can draw to SVG (like D3.js), Canvas (like p5.js), or WebGL (like Three.js) using the same API, which makes it great for projects that might benefit from being able to migrate between these different contexts with a single code base.
info.title: getting started
global vars
info.text: We start by creating a new Two() instance, which acts as both our drawing context and animation loop. We'll also create a group variable which will contain a couple of different shapes we want to access to in both our functions.

info.title: append to body
info.text: When we call two.appendTo(document.body) inside our setup, Two creates its own render surface (an SVG or canvas element) and inserts it into the page. From there, every shape we make is managed by Two’s internal scene graph.

info.title: shapes
info.text: Inside setup we'll also create two shapes: a circle and a rectangle. We use .makeCircle() to create a circle, setting it's x position to -70 and giving it a radius of 50. We'll also adjust the default position of the rectangle we created with .makeRectangle() by 70 and set both it's width and height to 100.

info.title: colors
info.text: We set a fill color for each directly using hex and RGBA string values.

info.title: group
info.text: Next, we find the center of the scene using two.width and two.height, and create a Group containing both shapes. This group allows us to move, scale, and rotate the two shapes together as one. We position it in the center, set its scale to 0 (so it starts invisible), and remove its stroke with group.noStroke().

info.title: bind animate
info.text: For animation, we bind our custom animate function to Two’s internal update event with two.bind('update', animate). Every time Two updates (roughly 60 frames per second), it calls this function.

info.title: animate
info.text: Inside the animate function we gradually increase group.scale until it reaches 1, while also rotating it. When the scale reaches full size, we reset both the scale and rotation back to 0, creating a continuous pulsating spin.

info.title: call play
info.text: The last line in our setup calls two.play() which starts the automatic update loop, driving our animation frame by frame.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded.

There’s loads more you can do with this library, this is only the beginning. Two.js has excellent documentation and examples. Two.js supports paths, polygons, text, gradients, and custom shaders. You can animate along curves, switch renderers on the fly, or even export shapes to SVG for editing in other software.
